message: I want to strengthen my legs
response_text: To strengthen your legs, aim for 2–3 focused sessions per week, using controlled reps and gradually increasing resistance over time. Great options to start with are the **glute bridge (two legs on bench)** for glutes/hamstrings and the **band two-legs calf raise** for your calves—try 3 sets of 8–12 reps each and add tension as it gets easier.

Here are a couple of great ab exercises you can start with: **Plank** (builds deep core stability—aim for 3 sets of 20–60 seconds) and **Bicycle Crunches** (targets upper/lower abs and obliques—try 3 sets of 12–20 reps per side). If you tell me your level (beginner/intermediate) and whether you have any back issues, I can tailor a short ab routine for you.

For your chest, great staples are **push-ups** (easy to scale by doing them on your knees or elevated on a bench) and the **bench press** (barbell or dumbbells) to build strength and size. If you want, tell me what equipment you have (none/dumbbells/gym) and your goal (strength vs muscle), and I’ll suggest a quick chest routine.

message: I want to train my arms
response_text: To train your arms, focus on a mix of pushing and pulling movements 2–3 times per week, leaving at least a day between sessions for recovery. Good staples are **bicep curls** (for biceps) and **tricep dips** (for triceps); aim for 3–4 sets of 8–12 reps and gradually increase weight or reps over time.

For your back, great go-to exercises are **lat pulldowns or pull-ups** (to build your lats) and **dumbbell/barbell rows** (to strengthen your mid-back). If you also want lower-back strength, add **back extensions** with controlled form and a neutral spine.

Here are a couple great shoulder exercises to get you started: **Dumbbell Shoulder Press** (builds overall shoulder strength) and **Lateral Raises** (targets the side delts for width). If you tell me your equipment (dumbbells, cables, bands, or none) and your goal (strength, size, rehab), I can tailor a short routine.

Here are a couple of great cardio options you can do with little to no equipment: **brisk walking or jogging** (easy to scale up or down) and **jump rope** (high-intensity and efficient). Aim for **20–30 minutes**, 3–5 days/week, and keep the intensity at a level where you can talk but feel slightly out of breath.

For beginners, aim for simple full‑body moves 2–3 times per week and focus on good form over heavy weight. Great starter exercises include bodyweight squats and modified push-ups; begin with 2–3 sets of 8–12 reps and increase gradually as they feel easier.

message: I need a full body workout
response_text: Here’s a simple full-body plan you can do anywhere: try a **push and pull bodyweight** circuit (push-ups + inverted rows or band rows), then add squats and a plank. Do 3 rounds of 8–12 reps per move (30–60s rest), and aim to repeat it 2–3 times per week.

For weight loss, prioritize exercises that burn lots of calories and build/maintain muscle: combine cardio (like brisk walking/running, cycling, rowing) with full‑body strength training. Great go-to options are interval training (HIIT) and compound lifts (like squats and deadlifts) a few times per week—this mix boosts calorie burn and helps your metabolism stay higher over time.

For upper body strength training, focus on a few big compound movements and progress the weight or reps gradually each week while keeping good form. A simple, effective start is **push-ups** (chest/shoulders/triceps) and **dumbbell or barbell rows** (back/biceps)—aim for 3–4 sets of 6–12 reps each, 2–3 times per week with at least a day of rest between sessions.

For core strengthening, focus on moves that train both stability and controlled movement. Great options are **planks** (front/side) and **dead bugs**—aim for 2–3 sets of 20–40 seconds (planks) and 8–12 reps per side (dead bugs), keeping your lower back neutral and breathing steadily.

What would you like help with today—workout plan, an exercise question (form, muscles worked, alternatives), or something like nutrition/recovery? If you tell me your goal (strength, fat loss, muscle gain), your equipment (gym/home), and any injuries, I’ll tailor a quick recommendation.

message: I want to workout but don't know what to do
response_text: A great place to start is a simple full-body routine 2–3 times per week: try **bodyweight squats** and **push-ups** (or incline push-ups), then add a **plank**—do 2–3 sets of 8–12 reps (30–45s for planks). If you tell me your goal (fat loss, muscle, strength), your experience level, and what equipment you have (none/dumbbells/gym), I can build a quick plan for you.

To get fit, aim for a simple weekly mix: 3–4 strength sessions plus 2–3 cardio sessions, and keep it consistent while gradually increasing weight, reps, or time. Great starting exercises are **squats** and **push-ups** (full-body strength), paired with **brisk walking or cycling** for cardio. If you tell me your goal (fat loss, muscle, endurance), current level, and how many days/week you can train, I’ll map out a quick plan.
